planting.note: Plant divisions (pups) after the last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Goldfinger is a FHIA-bred hybrid known for disease resistance and acceptable cold hardiness, with rhizomes surviving light freezes in zone 9 and reliably resprouting in spring.

directions: Plant divisions (pups) after last frost in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; Goldfinger banana (FHIA-01) is a tetraploid hybrid developed by the Honduran Foundation for Agricultural Research (FHIA) in the 1980s, bred specifically for resistance to Black Sigatoka leaf disease, Fusarium wilt (Panama disease), and moko bacterial wilt — the three most destructive banana pathogens — making it a significant improvement over susceptible Cavendish varieties for home gardeners in humid subtropical climates. The fruit clusters are large, with medium-sized, slightly angular fruits ripening yellow that have sweet, full banana flavor with a hint of apple when fully ripe; texture is firmer than Cavendish and the fruits resist bruising and handle well. Goldfinger is productive (producing 40–70 pounds of fruit per pseudostem) and regrows quickly from the rhizome after freeze dieback in zone 8b–9, typically reaching fruiting size within 12–18 months of resprouting. An excellent choice for Gulf South food gardens where disease pressure is high and cold resilience is valued.
sources: Honduras Foundation for Agricultural Research (FHIA) technical bulletins

planting.note: Plant divisions (pups) after the last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Manzano is less cold-hardy than Cavendish or Orinoco types and is best suited to zone 9b–11 or container culture in cooler climates where it can be overwintered indoors.

directions: Plant divisions (pups) after last frost in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; Manzano banana (also called Apple Banana, Finger Banana, or 'Bocadillo' in Colombia) is a widely grown short-fruited banana popular throughout Latin America and the Caribbean, producing plump, stubby fruits (3–5 inches long, broader in proportion than Cavendish) with a distinctive, complex flavor reminiscent of strawberry and apple when fully ripe — a significantly more aromatic and interesting eating experience than a standard Cavendish. The fruits turn bright yellow and then develop black skin patches as they ripen, and the flesh remains firm even when sweet and fully ripe, giving them better shelf life than many banana types. Manzano banana plants are more compact than Cavendish (8–12 feet versus 12–20 feet) making them practical for smaller yards. Manzano is moderately susceptible to Black Sigatoka and should be grown in well-drained, well-fertilized conditions with regular irrigation; it thrives in Gulf Coast conditions (zones 9b–11) but pseudostems are killed by even brief freezes.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center
Purdue University Center for New Crops

common_name: Lady Finger Banana (Sugar Banana, Fig Banana)

planting.note: Plant divisions (pups) after the last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Lady Finger banana performs best in zones 9b–11 and needs frost protection in zone 9a, though the rhizome may survive light freezes with heavy mulching.

directions: Plant divisions (pups) after last frost in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; Lady Finger banana (also called Sugar Banana, Sucrier, or 'Fig Banana') is an AAB genome group banana producing long, thin, elegant fruits (4–5 inches long but unusually slender) with delicate, thin, easily peeled skin and exceptionally sweet, rich, aromatic flavor — widely regarded as one of the finest-flavored fresh-eating bananas available, with a honey-like sweetness and floral aroma that surpasses standard Cavendish bananas. The fruits are produced in large bunches of 12–20 fingers per hand, and the delicate thin skin means they do not ship well commercially (hence their relative scarcity in supermarkets despite their superior flavor), making Lady Finger an excellent choice for the home gardener who wants superior banana flavor and can eat the fruits promptly at ripeness. The tall (12–18 foot) plants produce large ornamental pseudostems with broad tropical leaves; they are truly tropical and suffer pseudostem dieback in any freeze but the rhizomes can survive to about 28–30°F with heavy mulching in zone 9b. Grow in the warmest, most protected microclimate available in zone 9.

planting.note: Plant divisions (pups) after the last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Namwa is an ABB genome banana with greater cold and drought tolerance than pure Cavendish types, and the rhizome reliably resurfaces after freeze damage in zone 8b–9.

directions: Plant divisions (pups) after last frost in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; Namwa (also spelled 'Nam Wa', 'Kluai Nam Wa' in Thai, meaning 'water banana') is an ABB genome banana that is among the most widely grown commercial and home garden bananas in Southeast Asia, particularly Thailand, where it is the dominant fresh market variety. Its great popularity stems from its compact size (8–12 feet tall), reliable productivity, good flavor (sweet, slightly tangy, slightly firmer than Cavendish), and significantly greater drought and cold tolerance than Cavendish types — the ABB genome contributes Musa balbisiana cold and stress resistance that allows the rhizome to survive temperatures to about 22–25°F, making Namwa useful in zone 8b–9 Gulf South gardens. The medium-sized, plump, round-tipped fruits (5–6 inches) ripen to creamy yellow with a thin skin and dense, sweet, slightly chalky-creamy flesh; they are excellent fresh, and the slightly starchy texture when green-ripe makes them suitable for cooking or frying as well. A productive, compact, and cold-resilient choice for Gulf South banana cultivation.
sources: Thailand Department of Agriculture Banana Research

planting.note: Plant divisions (pups) after the last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Burro is suited to zones 9b–12 and does not tolerate hard freezes well, though the rhizome may survive brief dips to 28–30°F with heavy mulching and a warm microclimate.

directions: Plant divisions (pups) after last frost in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; Burro banana (also called 'Chunky Banana', 'Horse Banana', or 'Bluggoe') is a distinctive ABB genome banana producing uniquely short, thick, blocky fruits (4–6 inches long but very broad and nearly square in cross-section with squared-off ridges) that stand out visually from the typical elongated banana shape. The fruits ripen from green to yellow and develop a characteristic tangy, lemon-tinged sweetness with a slightly chewy texture when fully ripe (skin may be greenish-yellow outside but flesh is cream-colored and sweet); when eaten green-ripe or cooked, the starchy texture is similar to plantain, making Burro a dual-purpose banana suited to both fresh eating when ripe and cooking applications when green. The trees are medium-large (10–14 feet) and vigorous in tropical to subtropical conditions, producing large bunches with up to 8 hands. Burro is well adapted to Gulf Coast humidity and heat in zones 9b–11 and is particularly popular in Mexican and Central American households for its unique dual-use character.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center
Purdue University Center for New Crops
